questions.subject: Physics
options: It is possible to have $|\vec{C}| < |\vec{A}|$ as well as $|\vec{C}| < |\vec{B}|$
questions.solution:
Given $\vec{C} = \vec{A} + \vec{B}$.

The magnitude of $\vec{C}$ is given by $|\vec{C}| = \sqrt{|\vec{A}|^2 + |\vec{B}|^2 + 2|\vec{A}||\vec{B}|\cos\theta}$, where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$.

If the angle between the vectors is obtuse ($\theta > 90^{\circ}$), the term $2|\vec{A}||\vec{B}|\cos\theta$ becomes negative, which can make $|\vec{C}|$ smaller than both $|\vec{A}|$ and $|\vec{B}|$.

For example, if $\vec{A} = 5\hat{i}$ and $\vec{B} = -6\hat{i}$, then $\vec{C} = -\hat{i}$.

Here, $|\vec{A}| = 5$, $|\vec{B}| = 6$, and $|\vec{C}| = 1$.

Thus, $|\vec{C}| < |\vec{A}|$ and $|\vec{C}| < |\vec{B}|$ is possible.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A physical scenario can be described by employing various sets of coordinate axes that are oriented differently. Which among the following do not depend on the orientation of these axes?
questions.solution:
A vector is a physical or geometric entity that exists independently of any coordinate system. Its magnitude and direction in space remain fixed regardless of the orientation of the axes.

The value of a scalar is a single numerical value that is invariant under the rotation of coordinate axes.

The magnitude of a vector is its length, which is a scalar quantity and remains invariant regardless of the orientation of the axes.

The components of a vector are its projections along the coordinate axes. When the axes are rotated, the projections change. Hence, only the components of a vector depend on the orientation of the axes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For the resultant of several vectors, its x-component
options: equals the sum of the x-components of the individual vectors
might be equal to the sum of the magnitudes of the vectors
might be less than the sum of the magnitudes of the vectors
might be greater than the sum of the magnitudes of the vectors
questions.solution:
Let the vectors be $\vec{A}_1, \vec{A}_2, \dots, \vec{A}_n$.

The resultant vector is $\vec{R} = \vec{A}_1 + \vec{A}_2 + \dots + \vec{A}_n$.

Taking the x-component on both sides, $R_x = A_{1x} + A_{2x} + \dots + A_{nx}$. Thus, the x-component of the resultant equals the sum of the x-components of the individual vectors.

The magnitude of the resultant satisfies $|\vec{R}| \le |\vec{A}_1| + |\vec{A}_2| + \dots + |\vec{A}_n|$.

Since the x-component of any vector cannot exceed its magnitude, $R_x \le |\vec{R}|$.

Combining these inequalities gives $R_x \le |\vec{A}_1| + |\vec{A}_2| + \dots + |\vec{A}_n|$.

If all vectors are directed along the positive x-axis, $R_x = |\vec{A}_1| + |\vec{A}_2| + \dots + |\vec{A}_n|$.

If the vectors are not all directed along the positive x-axis, $R_x < |\vec{A}_1| + |\vec{A}_2| + \dots + |\vec{A}_n|$.

Therefore, the x-component of the resultant might be equal to or less than the sum of the magnitudes of the vectors, but it can never be greater.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose the angle between two nonzero vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ is $120^\circ$, and let their resultant be $\vec{C}$.
questions.solution:
The magnitude of the resultant vector $\vec{C}$ is given by:

$C = |\vec{A} + \vec{B}| = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + 2AB \cos \theta}$

Given $\theta = 120^{\circ}$, $\cos 120^{\circ} = -\dfrac{1}{2}$. Substituting this value:

$C = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 - AB}$

The expression for $|A - B|$ can be written as:

$|A - B| = \sqrt{(A - B)^2} = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 - 2AB}$

Since $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ are nonzero vectors, their magnitudes $A$ and $B$ are strictly positive, which means $AB > 0$.

Comparing the terms inside the square roots:

$A^2 + B^2 - AB > A^2 + B^2 - 2AB$

Taking the square root of both sides yields:

$\sqrt{A^2 + B^2 - AB} > \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 - 2AB}$

$C > |A - B|$

Thus, $C$ is necessarily greater than $|A - B|$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$, the magnitude of their vector product can be
questions.solution:
The magnitude of the vector product of two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ is given by $|\vec{A} \times \vec{B}| = AB \sin \theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the vectors.

Since the value of $\sin \theta$ lies in the range $[0, 1]$ for $\theta \in [0, \pi]$, we have $0 \le \sin \theta \le 1$.

Multiplying by $AB$, we get $0 \le AB \sin \theta \le AB$.

Thus, the magnitude of the vector product can be zero (if $\theta = 0$ or $\pi$), less than $AB$ (if $0 < \theta < \pi/2$ or $\pi/2 < \theta < \pi$), or equal to $AB$ (if $\theta = \pi/2$).

It can never be greater than $AB$.
